1: shadow Vindicate me, O God, and defend my cause against an ungodly people; from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me! Christ's cry for vindication against those who condemned him unjustly. God's vindication comes through resurrection: declared Son of God in power (Romans 1:4). The deceitful witnesses and unjust Pilate are overruled by the verdict of the empty tomb.
2: shadow For you are the God in whom I take refuge; why have you rejected me? Why do I go about mourning because of the oppression of the enemy? The paradox of the cross — taking refuge in the God who appears to have rejected — is Christ's own experience on Calvary (Psalms 22:1; Matthew 27:46). The mourning of desolation gives way to the joy of resurrection.
3: direct Send out your light and your truth; let them lead me; let them bring me to your holy hill and to your dwelling! Christ is both the light sent and the truth sent: I am the way, and the truth, and the life (John 14:6); the true light, which gives light to everyone, was coming into the world (John 1:9). The Father sends his own Son as light and truth to lead his people to the holy mountain of his presence.
4: shadow Then I will go to the altar of God, to God my exceeding joy, and I will praise you with the lyre, O God, my God. Christ is simultaneously the worshiper and the altar: we have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat (Hebrews 13:10). He is the exceeding joy the soul reaches when it comes to God — in your presence there is fullness of joy (Psalms 16:11).
5: shadow Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in God; for I shall again praise him, my salvation and my God. The threefold refrain (Psalms 42:5, Psalms 42:11, Psalms 43:5) structures Christ's movement through the Passion: soul cast down in Gethsemane, turbulent at the cross, yet commanding hope in God. The resurrection is the praise that answers all three laments.
